summary: Build a Hermes-native Community integration line from the existing OpenClaw CommunityIntegrationSkill capabilities, then validate a fresh Hermes agent/profile against the current Community server contract without destabilizing the merged OpenClaw baseline.

in_scope: Design and implement a Hermes-side Community integration artifact that preserves the current Community onboarding, session/sync, state, send-route, and canonical-effect contract.
Reuse framework-agnostic logic from the current OpenClaw skill where it is still correct, but remove OpenClaw-specific filesystem, config, and service assumptions.
Run fresh Hermes validation on a fresh isolated Hermes profile/environment, then report success or the first real blocker with evidence.
Escalate live or server-host validation through the project-standard HTTP-controller path if local Linux validation is not sufficient.
out_of_scope: Changing the Community server contract just to fit Hermes.
Reopening or destabilizing the already-merged OpenClaw release baseline as part of the first Hermes spike.
Pretending a plain Hermes skill alone is enough when the work actually needs a plugin/tool plus runtime bridge.

key_conclusions: This should not be implemented as a plain Hermes skill only. Hermes official docs state that skills are for instructions plus existing tools, while auth flows, API-keyed integrations, multi-component configuration, and real-time events belong in a Tool or Plugin.

The Hermes-native shape should be: Hermes plugin for precise tools and stateful operations, bundled Hermes skill for operator workflow guidance, and a thin local bridge for any Community webhook/send contract Hermes does not natively match.

Preserve the current Community-side contract first. Do not weaken signature validation, silently drop the active send route, or rename the Community webhook shape just because Hermes has different defaults.
Keep the current OpenClaw line isolated. A first Hermes implementation should be developed in an isolated work root or separate artifact path, then merged intentionally only after validation passes.

authoritative_inputs.purpose: Repo ownership and the rule that openclaw-for-community is template/reference, not the release repo.
Stable acceptance layers and the required fresh-onboarding validation model.
Current skill-body entrypoints and existing formal tests.

authoritative_inputs.path: G:\community agnts\community agents\myproject\docs\design-facts\Agent Community Skill设计文档.txt
authoritative_inputs.purpose: Skill boundary: onboarding/update/access prep and canonical message packaging belong in the skill/integration layer.

authoritative_inputs.path: G:\community agnts\community agents\myproject\docs\design-facts\Agent Community Onboarding与Session合同设计文档.txt
authoritative_inputs.purpose: Fresh-install success means identity plus session/sync plus local state plus send route plus canonical effect.
Hermes official rule for when work should be a Skill versus a Tool.
Hermes official plugin mechanism, including plugin-bundled skills and env gating.
Fresh isolated Hermes profiles for clean validation.
Hermes webhook capabilities and limits; compare carefully against the current Community webhook contract.
Hermes official OpenClaw migration model and destination paths for imported skills.

current_code_evidence.finding: Current CLI probes .openclaw, openclaw.json, and OpenClaw model/provider layout directly; this cannot be carried into Hermes unchanged.
Current onboarding path is tightly coupled to Linux systemd, OpenClaw ingress/service names, .openclaw paths, Unix socket layout, and port-8848 ingress management.
Current runtime exposes both /webhook/<agent_handle> and /send/<agent_handle>, validates x-community-webhook-signature, and persists Community state under template/state paths.
Existing tests already encode canonical outbound message shape and representative inbound required-reply behavior; this is reusable acceptance logic.
Existing runtime tests encode obligation classification semantics that should remain stable across agent frameworks.

environment_facts: This discussion thread is running in Windows PowerShell under G:\community agnts.
Hermes CLI is not installed on the Windows host shell.
WSL2 Ubuntu exists locally and can be used as the first realistic Linux validation surface.
Hermes CLI is not currently installed inside WSL Ubuntu either.

The community-skill repo currently has user changes in scripts/community_integration.mjs, scripts/ensure-community-agent-onboarding.sh, and tests/community-skill-outbound-v2.test.mjs; do not revert unrelated work.

artifact_shape: Hermes plugin: owns exact Community API operations, saved state, onboarding/profile tools, and any env-gated credentials.
Bundled Hermes skill: tells Hermes when and how to use the plugin tools for Community onboarding, status, profile sync, and structured reply flows.
Thin bridge process: preserves any Community contract Hermes does not natively match, especially exact webhook path/header behavior and the active local send route.
why_not_plain_skill: Community integration needs auth flows and saved tokens.
Community integration needs precise request shaping every time.
Community integration needs real-time inbound webhook handling and active send routing.
Hermes official docs classify those needs under Tool/Plugin, not plain skill-only behavior.
reuse_plan: Reuse Community API request and canonical message normalization logic where it is framework-agnostic.
Reuse acceptance semantics from the existing Node tests instead of inventing a new success definition.
Do not reuse .openclaw paths, OpenClaw config probing, service names, or template directory assumptions.
contract_preservation_rules: Preserve Community ingress expectation on port 8848 unless there is explicit new evidence that the contract has changed.
Do not silently swap x-community-webhook-signature for a different header without an adapter or contract update.
Do not silently drop /send/<agent_handle>; if Hermes-native tooling cannot provide it directly, implement an adapter/bridge.

ownership.reason: This work is high-coupling backend/skill/runtime integration and should not be split into frontend work.
WSL2 Ubuntu is available and is the fastest first validation surface.
Real server-side validation, deployment, or environment repair should still go through the HTTP controller path.

implementation_strategy.suggested_work_root: Use an isolated Hermes-specific local path or worktree first instead of modifying the merged OpenClaw release path in place.

stages.title: Prepare Linux-capable Hermes environment
actions: Use WSL2 Ubuntu or another Linux host.
Install Hermes CLI and verify hermes commands actually run.
Record exact install steps and versions used for reproducibility.
acceptance: hermes --version works in the chosen Linux environment.
hermes profile create works in the chosen Linux environment.

stages.title: Create Hermes-native integration shape
actions: Create a Hermes plugin directory and register plugin tools for onboarding, status, send, profile sync, and profile update.
Add a bundled Hermes skill that calls those tools instead of embedding fragile prose-only logic.
Implement a thin bridge for Community webhook/send compatibility where Hermes native webhook behavior is insufficient.
acceptance: There is a clearly loadable Hermes plugin.
There is a clearly loadable Hermes skill or plugin-bundled skill.
OpenClaw-specific config assumptions are removed from the Hermes line.

stages.title: Port and run local formal tests
actions: Port or adapt the reusable canonical-message and runtime-obligation tests.
Add Hermes-side tests for saved state location and bridge behavior where needed.
Report exactly which test layer passed.
acceptance: Repository-local tests pass for the Hermes integration line.
Canonical outbound shape and representative inbound handling are covered.

stages.title: Run fresh Hermes profile validation
actions: Create a fresh isolated Hermes profile for the test. Prefer clone-based profile creation if model/API config reuse is needed, but keep Community state fresh.
Install or register the Hermes integration artifact in that fresh profile.
Run onboarding against the current Community server.
Verify identity, webhook or equivalent inbound path, session/sync, saved local state, active send route, and canonical effect.
acceptance: Fresh Hermes profile onboarding succeeds under the formal Community success definition, not just partial identity creation.
At least one representative inbound-to-canonical-effect case succeeds.

stages.title: Escalate only if real-environment proof is still needed
actions: If local Linux validation is not enough, create a main-controller or live JSON task for real server-side validation.
Attach evidence paths and logs instead of loose prose.
acceptance: Any live or server result is backed by structured evidence and log paths.

fresh_hermes_validation_definition: Webhook or equivalent inbound subscription is active under the current Community contract.
Recoverable local Community state is written under the Hermes-side environment/profile.
An inbound Community message can produce canonical effect, not just HTTP acceptance.
acceptance: Do not report success on identity-only creation.
Do not report success on webhook 202 or listener health alone.
Do not report success on service active alone.
Report syntax or lint, repository-local tests, local Linux validation, and any live validation as separate layers.
If the bridge is required for exact Community compatibility, include a direct proof that it preserves the expected webhook header and route behavior.

blocker_rules.when: Hermes is not installed in the chosen Linux environment
blocker_rules.action: Treat it as the first real blocker, install or document installation failure, then continue.

blocker_rules.when: Hermes native webhook support does not match the Community header or path contract
blocker_rules.action: Add a thin adapter or bridge; do not silently weaken auth or mutate the server contract.

blocker_rules.when: The only evidence is HTTP acceptance, route reachability, or a running process
blocker_rules.action: Do not mark success; continue until session/sync, saved state, send route, and canonical effect are proven or a blocker is reached.

blocker_rules.when: A proposed Hermes-side change would destabilize the already-merged OpenClaw release line
blocker_rules.action: Keep the Hermes line isolated and surface the integration boundary explicitly.

deliverables: Short implementation summary: what was built and why this shape was chosen.
Exact local Linux validation commands and results.
Exact fresh Hermes profile creation method used for the test.
Evidence for identity, session/sync, local state, send route, and canonical effect.
If blocked, the first real blocker plus the next safe step.
